title: Break apart 2-digit addition problems
description: Practice breaking apart problems like 23+45 into problems like 20+40+3+5.

assessment_items.question_content:
Joel isn't sure how to add $65+17$.

**Help Joel by choosing an addition problem that is  the same as $65+17$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can think of $\purpleC{65}$ as a $\purpleC{60}$ and a $\purpleC{5}$.  

We can think of $\blueD{17}$ as a $\blueD{10}$ and a $\blueD{7}$.

$\phantom{=}\purpleC{65}+\blueD{17}$

$=\purpleC{60}+\purpleC{5}+\blueD{10}+\blueD{7}$

$= \purpleC{60}+\blueD{10}+\purpleC{5}+\blueD{7}$
The answer: $60+10+5+7$

assessment_items.question_content:
Lindsay isn't sure how to add $39+61$.

**Help Lindsay by choosing an addition problem that is  the same as $39+61$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can think of $\purpleC{39}$ as a $\purpleC{30}$ and a $\purpleC{9}$.  

We can think of $\blueD{61}$ as a $\blueD{60}$ and a $\blueD{1}$.

$\phantom{=}\purpleC{39}+\blueD{61}$

$=\purpleC{30}+\purpleC{9}+\blueD{60}+\blueD{1}$

$= \purpleC{30}+\blueD{60}+\purpleC{9}+\blueD{1}$
The answer: $30+60+9+1$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which addition problem is the same as $52+45$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can think of $\purpleC{52}$ as a $\purpleC{50}$ and a $\purpleC{2}$.  

We can think of $\blueD{45}$ as a $\blueD{40}$ and a $\blueD{5}$.

$\phantom{=}\purpleC{52}+\blueD{45}$

$=\purpleC{50}+\purpleC{2}+\blueD{40}+\blueD{5}$

$= \purpleC{50}+\blueD{40}+\purpleC{2}+\blueD{5}$
The answer: $50+40+2+5$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which addition problem is the same as $7+73$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can think of $\blueD{73}$ as a $\blueD{70}$ and a $\blueD{3}$.  
$\phantom{=}\purpleC{7}+\blueD{73}$

$=\blueD{73}+\purpleC{7}$

$=\blueD{70}+\blueD{3}+\purpleC{7}$
The answer: $70+3+7$

assessment_items.question_content:
**What do we get when we break apart $18+70$ using place value?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We can think of $\purpleC{18}$ as a $\purpleC{10}$ and a $\purpleC{8}$.

$\phantom{=}\purpleC{18}+\blueD{70}$

$=\blueD{70}+\purpleC{18}$

$= \blueD{70}+\purpleC{10}+\purpleC{8}$
The answer: $70+10+8$

assessment_items.question_content:
**What do we get when we break apart $46+26$ using place value?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can think of $\purpleC{46}$ as a $\purpleC{40}$ and a $\purpleC{6}$.  

We can think of $\blueD{26}$ as a $\blueD{20}$ and a $\blueD{6}$.

$\phantom{=}\purpleC{46}+\blueD{26}$

$=\purpleC{40}+\purpleC{6}+\blueD{20}+\blueD{6}$

$= \purpleC{40}+\blueD{20}+\purpleC{6}+\blueD{6}$
The answer: $40+20+6+6$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which expression could be used to find the value of $73+24$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can think of $\purpleC{73}$ as a $\purpleC{70}$ and a $\purpleC{3}$.  

We can think of $\blueD{24}$ as a $\blueD{20}$ and a $\blueD{4}$.

$\phantom{=}\purpleC{73}+\blueD{24}$

$=\purpleC{70}+\purpleC{3}+\blueD{20}+\blueD{4}$

$= \purpleC{70}+\blueD{20}+\purpleC{3}+\blueD{4}$
The answer: $70+20+3+4$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which expression could be used to find the value of $34+59$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can think of $\purpleC{34}$ as a $\purpleC{30}$ and a $\purpleC{4}$.  

We can think of $\blueD{59}$ as a $\blueD{50}$ and a $\blueD{9}$.

$\phantom{=}\purpleC{34}+\blueD{59}$

$=\purpleC{30}+\purpleC{4}+\blueD{50}+\blueD{9}$

$= \purpleC{30}+\blueD{50}+\purpleC{4}+\blueD{9}$
The answer: $30+50+4+9$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is equal to $20+32$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can think of $\blueD{32}$ as a $\blueD{30}$ and a $\blueD{2}$.  
$\phantom{=}\purpleC{20}+\blueD{32}$

$=\purpleC{20}+\blueD{30}+\blueD{2}$
The answer: $20+30+2$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is equal to $89+8$ ?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can think of $\purpleC{89}$ as an $\purpleC{80}$ and a $\purpleC{9}$.  
$\phantom{=}\purpleC{89}+\blueD{8}$

$=\purpleC{80}+\purpleC{9}+\blueD{8}$
The answer: $80+9+8$

assessment_items.question_content:
Thomas isn't sure how to add $54+22$.

**Help Thomas by choosing an addition problem that is  the same as $54+22$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can think of $\purpleC{54}$ as $\purpleC{5\text{ tens}}$ and $\purpleC{4 \text{ ones}}$.  

We can think of $\blueD{22}$ as $\blueD{2\text{ tens}}$ and $\blueD{2\text{ ones}}$.

$\phantom{=}\purpleC{54}+\blueD{22}$

$=\purpleC{5\text{ tens}}+\purpleC{4\text{ ones}}+\blueD{2\text{ tens}}+\blueD{2\text{ ones}}$

$= \purpleC{5\text{ tens}}+\blueD{2\text{ tens}}+\purpleC{4\text{ ones}}+\blueD{2\text{ ones}}$
The answer: $5 \text{ tens}+2\text{ tens} +4 \text{ ones}+2 \text{ ones}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Mia isn't sure how to add $77+5$.

**Help Mia by choosing an addition problem that is  the same as $77+5$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can think of $\purpleC{77}$ as $\purpleC{7\text{ tens}}$ and $\purpleC{7 \text{ ones}}$.  

We can think of $\blueD{5}$ as $\blueD{5\text{ ones}}$.

$\phantom{=}\purpleC{77}+\blueD{5}$

$=\purpleC{7\text{ tens}}+\purpleC{7\text{ ones}}+\blueD{5\text{ ones}}$
The answer: $7 \text{ tens}+7\text{ ones}+5 \text{ ones}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which addition problem is the same as $41+57$ ?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can think of $\purpleC{41}$ as $\purpleC{4\text{ tens}}$ and $\purpleC{1 \text{ one}}$.  

We can think of $\blueD{57}$ as $\blueD{5\text{ tens}}$ and $\blueD{7\text{ ones}}$.

$\phantom{=}\purpleC{41}+\blueD{57}$

$=\purpleC{4\text{ tens}}+\purpleC{1\text{ one}}+\blueD{5\text{ tens}}+\blueD{7\text{ ones}}$

$= \purpleC{4\text{ tens}}+\blueD{5\text{ tens}}+\purpleC{1\text{ one}}+\blueD{7\text{ ones}}$
The answer: $4 \text{ tens}+5\text{ tens} +1 \text{ one}+7 \text{ ones}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which addition problem is the same as $39+60$ ?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can think of $\purpleC{39}$ as $\purpleC{3\text{ tens}}$ and $\purpleC{9 \text{ ones}}$.  

We can think of $\blueD{60}$ as $\blueD{6\text{ tens}}$.

$\phantom{=}\purpleC{39}+\blueD{60}$

$=\purpleC{3\text{ tens}}+\purpleC{9\text{ ones}}+\blueD{6\text{ tens}}$

$= \purpleC{3\text{ tens}}+\blueD{6\text{ tens}}+\purpleC{9\text{ ones}}$
The answer: $3 \text{ tens}+6\text{ tens} +9 \text{ ones}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**What do we get when we break apart $35+16$ using place value?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can think of $\purpleC{35}$ as $\purpleC{3\text{ tens}}$ and $\purpleC{5 \text{ ones}}$.  

We can think of $\blueD{16}$ as $\blueD{1\text{ ten}}$ and $\blueD{6\text{ ones}}$.

$\phantom{=}\purpleC{35}+\blueD{16}$

$=\purpleC{3\text{ tens}}+\purpleC{5\text{ ones}}+\blueD{1\text{ ten}}+\blueD{6\text{ ones}}$

$= \purpleC{3\text{ tens}}+\blueD{1\text{ ten}}+\purpleC{5\text{ ones}}+\blueD{6\text{ ones}}$
The answer: $3 \text{ tens}+1\text{ ten} +5\text{ ones}+6 \text{ ones}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**What do we get when we break apart $28+72$ using place value?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can think of $\purpleC{28}$ as $\purpleC{2\text{ tens}}$ and $\purpleC{8 \text{ ones}}$.  

We can think of $\blueD{72}$ as $\blueD{7\text{ tens}}$ and $\blueD{2\text{ ones}}$.

$\phantom{=}\purpleC{28}+\blueD{72}$

$=\purpleC{2\text{ tens}}+\purpleC{8\text{ ones}}+\blueD{7\text{ tens}}+\blueD{2\text{ ones}}$

$= \purpleC{2\text{ tens}}+\blueD{7\text{ tens}}+\purpleC{8\text{ ones}}+\blueD{2\text{ ones}}$
The answer: $2 \text{ tens}+7\text{ tens} +8 \text{ ones}+2 \text{ ones}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which expression could be used to find the value of $40+43$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can think of $\purpleC{40}$ as $\purpleC{4\text{ tens}}$.  

We can think of $\blueD{43}$ as $\blueD{4\text{ tens}}$ and $\blueD{3\text{ ones}}$.

$\phantom{=}\purpleC{40}+\blueD{43}$

$=\purpleC{4\text{ tens}}+\blueD{4\text{ tens}}+\blueD{3\text{ ones}}$
The answer: $4 \text{ tens}+4\text{ tens} +3 \text{ ones}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which expression could be used to find the value of $12+84$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can think of $\purpleC{12}$ as $\purpleC{1\text{ ten}}$ and $\purpleC{2 \text{ ones}}$.  

We can think of $\blueD{84}$ as $\blueD{8\text{ tens}}$ and $\blueD{4\text{ ones}}$.

$\phantom{=}\purpleC{12}+\blueD{84}$

$=\purpleC{1\text{ ten}}+\purpleC{2\text{ ones}}+\blueD{8\text{ tens}}+\blueD{4\text{ ones}}$

$= \purpleC{1\text{ ten}}+\blueD{8\text{ tens}}+\purpleC{2\text{ ones}}+\blueD{4\text{ ones}}$
The answer: $1 \text{ ten}+8\text{ tens} +2 \text{ ones}+4 \text{ ones}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is equal to $9+38$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can think of $\purpleC{9}$ as $\purpleC{9 \text{ ones}}$.  

We can think of $\blueD{38}$ as $\blueD{3\text{ tens}}$ and $\blueD{8\text{ ones}}$.

$\phantom{=}\purpleC{9}+\blueD{38}$

$=\purpleC{9\text{ ones}}+\blueD{3\text{ tens}}+\blueD{8\text{ ones}}$

$= \blueD{3\text{ tens}}+\blueD{8\text{ ones}}+\purpleC{9\text{ ones}}$
The answer: $3 \text{ tens}+8 \text{ ones}+9 \text{ ones}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is equal to $63+11$ ?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can think of $\purpleC{63}$ as $\purpleC{6\text{ tens}}$ and $\purpleC{3 \text{ ones}}$.  

We can think of $\blueD{11}$ as $\blueD{1\text{ ten}}$ and $\blueD{1\text{ one}}$.

$\phantom{=}\purpleC{63}+\blueD{11}$

$=\purpleC{6\text{ tens}}+\purpleC{3\text{ ones}}+\blueD{1\text{ ten}}+\blueD{1\text{ one}}$

$= \purpleC{6\text{ tens}}+\blueD{1\text{ ten}}+\purpleC{3\text{ ones}}+\blueD{1\text{ one}}$
The answer: $6 \text{ tens}+1\text{ ten} +3\text{ ones}+1 \text{ one}$
